text: Two ports are connected to the Vinci's operative arms: one for the robotic endoscope and another for surgical assistance, facilitating the robot-assisted minimally invasive procedure.

The surgeon mobilizes the left colonic flexure by sectioning the gastrocolic ligament, entering the lesser sac. Short gastric vessels are coagulated with bipolar devices and sectioned using ultrasonic shears. The splenic artery is dissected, and a vascular loop is placed around it to prevent direct manipulation.
The surgeon uses an endoscopic vascular clamp to occlude the splenic artery, aiming to reduce the risk of bleeding and decrease the spleen's volume during the section.

The surgeon identifies and preserves two branches supplying the lower pole of the spleen while clipping the trunk vascularizing the upper pole. The vascular clamp is removed, and the main trunk of the splenic vein is dissected. A vascular loop is placed to facilitate the dissection, and the tail of the pancreas is completely detached from the ascending part of the splenic hilum.

The surgeon clips and transects the identified upper pole artery and the superior branch of the splenic vein. The superior transected branches of the splenic hilum are freed from the upper surface of the pancreatic tail. Following the devascularization of the upper pole, the demarcation line is clearly visible, allowing the completion of the parenchymal transection using ultrasonic shears.

The surgeon uses bipolar forceps to achieve hemostasis on the transected splenic parenchyma. The splenoprenic ligaments are sectioned, allowing the transected spleen to be completely freed from surrounding attachments.

The surgeon removes the vascular loops and places the transected spleen into a bag for extraction through a small suprapubic incision. The splenophenic ligament is reconstructed to prevent torsion of the remaining spleen on its vascular pedicle.
A hemostatic matrix is applied to the dissected splenic parenchyma. A small drainage tube is visibly left in place.
